---
title: "26. Claim–Evidence Tables"
---

[![Open the lab in Colab](https://colab.research.google.com/assets/colab-badge.svg)](https://colab.research.google.com/github/davi-moreira/2026F_evidence_driven_research_purdue_HONR464/blob/main/notebooks/student/nb10_poster_criticism_lock_student.ipynb){target="_blank"}

> **The research decision.** For every claim your poster makes, decide which exact
> piece of evidence backs it, where that evidence lives, and what it does not
> establish, and record all three in one row of a claim–evidence table before the
> poster is locked.

## Why this decision matters

> *"I point at one number on your board and ask where it came from. Walk me to the
> line that made it and I trust the whole poster. Say 'it's in the analysis
> somewhere' and I stop believing every other number up there."*
> — a **research-integrity judge** working a poster hall

A poster is a set of promises made in public. Each headline number, each figure,
each cited fact is a claim a stranger can test in ninety seconds. The dangerous gap
is between a claim you can *say* and a claim you can *back*. A claim–evidence table
closes that gap on purpose, before anyone points a finger, so the honesty is built
into the board and not patched under pressure.

## The concept

A **claim–evidence table** is a table with one row per claim, pairing each claim
your poster makes with the specific evidence behind it. Example: a row that reads
"Material B conducts heat about 1.4x faster than Material A" in the claim column,
with the two measured conductivities in the evidence column.

Four columns carry each row, and each is a term worth knowing.

- **Claim.** The statement your poster asserts as true. Example: "our measured
  value of *g* is 9.79 m/s²."
- **Evidence.** The exact result or source that backs the claim, with its
  uncertainty. Example: 30 timed swings giving a period of 2.007 s, and the
  interval ±0.06 m/s² around the estimate.
- **Trace.** Where a number was actually produced, named precisely enough to open.
  A **traceable number** is one you can walk back to the notebook cell that computed
  it. Example: "cell 16, where `g = 4*pi**2*L / T**2` runs." A number you cannot
  walk back, one typed from memory or rounded by hand, is an **untraceable number**,
  and it does not belong on the board.
- **Boundary.** The **claim boundary** is the line between what the evidence
  licenses and what it does not. Example: "measured at one location, one pendulum
  length, timed by hand, so this is not the standard sea-level value."

Building the table is the graded skill. Once every claim has a filled row, an
unbacked claim has nowhere to hide, because a blank cell is a visible hole.

## A worked example

You built a physics poster reporting a measurement of *g*, the acceleration due to
gravity, from a simple pendulum. Your headline reads "we measured *g* = 9.79 m/s²."
Before the file goes to the printer, you build the table.

**Row 1 (the headline).** Claim: "*g* = 9.79 ± 0.06 m/s²." Evidence: 30 timings of a
1.00 m pendulum, mean period 2.007 s, the spread of the 30 periods giving the
interval. Trace: cell 16, the line `g = 4*pi**2*L / T**2`. Boundary: one location,
one length, hand timing, so a reaction-time bias of order a tenth of a second is
possible and the value is not claimed to be the accepted 9.81 m/s².

Filling Row 1 forces one honest catch. You reach for the trace and find the number
on your draft board reads 9.81, not 9.79. You had quietly written the textbook value
instead of the one your cell printed. The table caught an untraceable number before
a judge did.

**Row 2 (a secondary claim).** Claim: "the period barely changes with amplitude for
small swings." Evidence: periods measured at 5° and 15° differ by less than your
timing spread. Trace: cell 18, the amplitude comparison. Boundary: only tested below
15°, so nothing is claimed about large swings.

Two claims, two rows, every number walked back to a cell, every boundary written
down. That table is the thing that lets your poster survive a pointed finger.

## The Colab laboratory

This chapter's lab is notebook **nb10 — Poster criticism + final poster lock**. Open
it in Colab with the badge above and work the cells alongside this chapter. There
you run the final-lock audit on a real poster: you trace each number to the cell
that produced it, confirm each citation resolves to a source you opened yourself,
and turn loose claims into locked, backed rows.

## Recommended AI prompts

Commit your own draft table first, then delegate. Each prompt is a checkable job,
not a request for a verdict.

**Locate the source for a cited fact.**

```text
Act as a physics reference assistant. I want to cite the accepted sea-level value
of g. Name the value and one real, retrievable source that reports it, and give me
enough detail to find it myself. If you are not confident a source exists, say so
instead of guessing.
```
After running, verify: open the named source yourself and confirm it reports that
value before it touches your poster. Counters *confident fabrication* (an invented
citation arrives as confidently as a real one).

**List every claim, so none is unbacked.**

```text
Here is my poster text: [paste it]. List every distinct claim it makes, one per
row. For each, name what evidence a reader would need to see to believe it. Do not
judge whether my evidence is good; just surface every claim, including implicit
ones.
```
After running, verify: check the list against your own table and add any claim you
had not written a row for. Counters *illusion of completeness* (a tidy list that
quietly skips the one claim with no evidence behind it).

**Red-team the claim–evidence match.**

```text
Here is one row of my claim-evidence table: claim "[paste]", evidence "[paste]".
Act as a hostile reviewer. Tell me every way this evidence fails to license this
claim, or claims more than it shows. Do not soften the claim for me; name the words
that overreach.
```
After running, verify: read each objection against your boundary column and reject
any that asks you to concede something your evidence does license. Counters *silent
scope change* (evidence for an easier claim, dressed up as backing the hard one).

::: {.callout-important title="Do not delegate"}
The **wording of each claim**, the **judgment that a piece of evidence actually
backs it**, and the **boundary each claim carries** stay yours. An AI can list
claims and draft rows, but it cannot open your notebook cell to confirm a number is
real, and it cannot decide the evidence is honest enough to print. The call that the
table is complete and the poster is locked is yours to declare and defend.
:::

## An AI failure case

You paste your poster text and ask an AI tool to build the whole claim–evidence
table for you. It returns a clean, complete grid, every cell filled, and the trace
column reads confidently: "*g* value from cell 14." You almost accept it. Then you
open cell 14, and it computes the *period*, not *g*. The value you report is
produced two cells later, in cell 16. The tool guessed a plausible cell number and
filled it with full confidence, and its tidy finished table hid the wrong trace
behind a polished look.

You catch it by refusing to trust a trace you have not opened. Reading the actual
cell, number in hand, is the whole discipline. A filled table is not a verified
table.

## Verification laboratory

Verify this chapter's key result, the table's traceability, with two named methods
from the [verification guide](https://github.com/davi-moreira/2026F_evidence_driven_research_purdue_HONR464/blob/main/ai_resources/verification_guide.md).
For every traced number, use **direct calculation**: recompute the value a second,
simpler way and confirm it matches the figure on the board, digit for digit. For
every cited fact, use **primary-source reading**: open the source, find the line,
and confirm it says what your poster claims. A number that survives an independent
recomputation and a citation that resolves to a real page are backed. A mismatch on
either is a finding, and the claim comes off the board until you fix it.

## Project transfer

This chapter is a graded piece of your **M10 final poster lock**. Build a
claim–evidence table for your own poster: one row for every headline number and
cited fact, each with its trace to a cell or source and its boundary written down.
That table is what you carry into the gallery-walk audit and the oral defense, and
it is the proof that your poster's honesty was verified, not merely asserted.

## Defend your decision

In four sentences, defend one row of your table. State the claim in the exact words
that will appear on your board, and name the specific evidence that backs it. State
its boundary: what this claim does *not* establish, and the compass position it
speaks from. Close with its uncertainty: the interval or caveat that rides in the
same eye-span as the claim, and the single check you ran to confirm the traced
number is real.
